article_meta.title: — News —Study Shows Pandemic Saw Rise In Firearm Injuries Among KidsBy Charlotte Pence Bond•Dec 20, 2022 DailyWire.com•F

transcript.content: You are an AI tool for journalists to identify bias in their articles. Analyze the article and return biased phrases, their bias types, and explanations. IMPORTANT ignore everything that quotes other people, only use what author is saying.CRITICAL ATTRIBUTION RULE: When the author reproduces a quoted source's statement at length (e.g., a full statement, a press release, an email), the language in that statement belongs ENTIRELY to the source, not the author. Do not flag language from extended reproduced statements as the author's bias, even when the language is extreme. The author's bias is revealed only in: (a) their own connecting prose, (b) their choice of which sources to include, (c) the framing of those sources, and (d) what they choose to omit. Output ONLY a valid JSON array. No preamble, no explanation outside the JSON, no markdown fences. If no bias is found, output an empty array: [] Format:[{"biasType": "Type of media bias", "biasedText": "Example from the article", "explanation": "explanation"}, {"biasType": "Type of media bias", "biasedText": "Example from the article", "explanation": "explanation"}] Example:[{"biasType": "Mind reading", "biasedText": "In a statement, Pillen characterized the NSEA's petition as an attack on children seeking the best education.", "explanation": "Assumes Pillen's view reflects the NSEA's intentions."}]

Types of media bias:- Spin: Vague or sensational language, including substituting an interpretive verb for a neutral one. Example: Writing "Senator X gloated over the bill's passage" rather than "said" or "responded" — the word 'gloated' implies smug pleasure without evidence, in the author's own voice.- Unsubstantiated claims: Claims without evidence.- Opinion Statements Presented as Fact: Subjective judgments presented as objective truth, without attribution. Example: A reporter writes in their own voice "The tax cuts failed to stimulate economic growth" — whether a policy "failed" is a contested  causal judgment that belongs in analysis, not unattributed news copy. - Sensationalism and Emotionalism: Information presented to shock.- Mudslinging/Ad Hominem: Unfair insults or attacks on character.- Mind reading: Assuming knowledge of others' thoughts.- Slant: Telling only part of the story present in the article — where information exists in the article but is structurally de-emphasized.   Example: An article reports a protester was injured prominently in the opening paragraphs, but mentions that three officers were also treated for injuries only in a single sentence near the end, attributed to a police statement. Both facts appear, but unequal prominence shapes the reader's impression. - Bias by Omission: Omitting information that would support an alternative viewpoint entirely — where the alternative perspective is absent from the article, not merely de-emphasized.   Example: An article on immigration enforcement quotes only affected families and advocacy organizations. No law enforcement perspective, border security rationale, or policy justification appears anywhere in the piece — the alternative viewpoint does not exist in the article at all. - Subjective Qualifying Adjectives: Bias revealed through adjectives.- Word Choice: Politically loaded words.- Negativity Bias: Emphasizing negative news.- Elite vs. Populist Bias: Deferring to prestigious institutions vs. dissenting perspectives. Exclude quotes from analysis. Be cautious and humble in your analysis. Present fewer, confident examples rather than more uncertain ones. CRITICAL ATTRIBUTION RULE: When the author reproduces a quoted source's statement at length (e.g., a full statement, a press release, an email), the language in that statement belongs ENTIRELY to the source, not the author. Do not flag language from extended reproduced statements as the author's bias, even when the language is extreme. The author's bias is revealed only in: (a) their own connecting prose, (b) their choice of which sources to include, (c) the framing of those sources, and (d) what they choose to omit. Analyze this article:

---
HEADLINE: — News —Study Shows Pandemic Saw Rise In Firearm Injuries Among KidsBy Charlotte Pence Bond•Dec 20, 2022 DailyWire.com•F
SOURCE: Morning Wire

ARTICLE:

— News —Study Shows Pandemic Saw Rise In Firearm Injuries Among KidsBy Charlotte Pence Bond•Dec 20, 2022 DailyWire.com•FacebookXMailListen to ArticleListen to this Article1xYour browser does not support the audio element.Emily Fennick \/ EyeEm via Getty Images A new study showed another disastrous effect of the pandemic on kids — a spike in gun injuries. The information comes alongside another study that showed the increasing homicide rates of kids.The study was published on Monday in JAMA Pediatrics and found that the rate of children with injuries from firearms going to children’s hospitals in the United States “significantly increased during the COVID-19 pandemic and remained elevated throughout 2021.”The study counted “all children younger than 18 years diagnosed with firearm injury,” using “data from the Pediatric Health Information System.”The research discovered that there were 1,815 firearm injuries in a given period prior to the pandemic, while there were 2,759 during the pandemic — which was a 52% spike.While there “were no significant differences between cohorts by sex, household income, rurality, region, mortality, or intent,” the study found that more non-Hispanic black children, kids ages 0 to 5 years old, as well as kids with public insurance were hurt by firearms during the pandemic when contrasted with kids harmed by firearms before the pandemic.“The COVID-19 pandemic was independently associated with increased monthly firearm injuries after controlling for all covariates,” it added.The monthly average of firearm injuries was also much higher during the period of the pandemic than it was prior to the pandemic.“This really highlights the fact that the leading cause of death in children and adolescents is firearms,” Stephanie Iantorno, a co-author of the analysis, said, per The Wall Street Journal.Data show that “in 2020 (the most recent year with available data from the CDC), firearms were the number one cause of death for children ages 1-19 in the United States, taking the lives of 4,357 children,” reports the Kaiser Family Foundation.“Researchers said psychological stress and other factors associated with the pandemic might have played a role in the rise in the incidence of firearm injuries and homicides among children,” WSJ notes.The homicide rates of children have also been going up in recent years, a separate study found. The rate has gone up each year, “on average 4.3% since 2013,” and it increased even more from 2019 to 2020, going up 27.7%.The study found that homicide rates have recently gone up a lot for males, black children, Hispanic kids, kids in the south, as well as those in urban and rural regions. They also went up for kids 6 to 10, 11 to 15, and 16 to 17 years of age.It also noted that homicide rates have gone down since 1999 for girls, infants, and children between the ages of one and five years old. It has also gone down for Asian or Pacific Islander children, white children, and kids in the Northeast.Dr. Elinore J. Kaufman, co-author of the editorial with the study, told The New York Times that homicides of younger kids typically happen inside or close to the home. They are most frequently carried out by people taking care of the children and parents and are also many times connected to neglect and child abuse and show the stressful situations of families, she said.“I don’t think we’re doing a good job of taking care of families, and it shows,” Dr. Kaufman noted.According to the Kaiser Family Foundation, the pandemic and the policies enacted as a result appear to have had a negative impact on children in multiple areas, including delays in preventative medical care, social isolation, and loss of school experience.Create a free account to join the conversation!Start CommentingAlready have an account?Log inGot a tip worth investigating?Your information could be the missing piece to an important story. Submit your tip today and make a difference.Submit TipThe Daily Wire > Read > Study Shows Pandemic Saw Rise In Firearm Injuries Among Kids
---

parsed_output.biasedText: A new study showed another disastrous effect of the pandemic on kids — a spike in gun injuries.
parsed_output.explanation: The author uses the term 'disastrous effect' and frames the increase as a 'spike,' which introduces emotional language and a sensational tone rather than simply reporting the increase.
